question: An inner product $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$ on a complex vector space satisfies three axioms. Together they make the space a Hilbert space (when complete). The inner product on $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ of two functions is:

options: Conjugate-linear in the first argument: $\langle\alpha f, g\rangle = \alpha^*\langle f,g\rangle$; linear in the second: $\langle f,\alpha g\rangle = \alpha\langle f,g\rangle$; and $\langle f,f\rangle \geq 0$ with equality iff $f = 0$
The inner product is linear in both arguments for complex spaces
$\langle f,g\rangle = \langle g,f\rangle$ always (without complex conjugation)
The inner product is only defined on finite-dimensional spaces
passive.explanation: The three inner product axioms: (1) conjugate symmetry $\langle f,g\rangle = \langle g,f\rangle^*$; (2) linearity in second argument; (3) positive definiteness. The induced norm $\|f\| = \sqrt{\langle f,f\rangle}$. In quantum mechanics: $\langle\psi,\phi\rangle = \langle\psi|\phi\rangle$ is the Dirac bra-ket notation. Normalisation requires $\langle\psi|\psi\rangle = 1$. The orthogonality of energy eigenstates: $\langle E_n|E_m\rangle = \delta_{nm}$.

active.prompt: Match each inner product property to its consequence in quantum mechanics:

pairs.definition: Probability interpretation: $|\langle\psi|\phi\rangle|^2 \in [0,1]$ is a valid probability
Heisenberg uncertainty: $\Delta A\,\Delta B \geq |\langle[\hat{A},\hat{B}]\rangle|/2$ follows from Cauchy-Schwarz
Every Cauchy sequence converges in the space; necessary for well-defined quantum dynamics
Resolution of identity: $\sum_n|e_n\rangle\langle e_n| = I$; any state $|\psi\rangle = \sum_n c_n|e_n\rangle$

active.explanation: The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality $|\langle f|g\rangle| \leq \|f\|\,\|g\|$ is the key inequality of Hilbert spaces. It gives the Robertson uncertainty relation when applied to $|f\rangle = \Delta\hat{A}|\psi\rangle$ and $|g\rangle = \Delta\hat{B}|\psi\rangle$.

question: The Gram-Schmidt process orthonormalises a set of linearly independent vectors $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots\}$ into an orthonormal set $\{e_1, e_2, \ldots\}$. The first two steps are:

options: $e_1 = v_1/\|v_1\|$; $u_2 = v_2 - \langle e_1, v_2\rangle e_1$ (remove component along $e_1$), then $e_2 = u_2/\|u_2\|$
$e_1 = v_1$; $e_2 = v_2 - v_1$ without normalisation
The process normalises each vector independently without removing parallel components
passive.explanation: Gram-Schmidt generates an orthonormal basis for the subspace spanned by $\{v_i\}$. The matrix $QR$ decomposition arises from applying Gram-Schmidt to the columns of $A$: $Q$ has the orthonormal columns, $R = Q^T A$ is upper triangular. In quantum chemistry, Gram-Schmidt orthogonalises atomic orbital basis functions to form Hartree-Fock molecular orbitals.

question: The adjoint (Hermitian conjugate) $A^\dagger$ of an operator $A$ on a Hilbert space is defined by $\langle A^\dagger f, g\rangle = \langle f, Ag\rangle$ for all $f, g$. Key classes:

options: Hermitian (self-adjoint): $A^\dagger = A$ → real eigenvalues, orthogonal eigenvectors (observables in QM); Anti-Hermitian: $A^\dagger = -A$ → purely imaginary eigenvalues; Unitary: $A^\dagger A = I$ → eigenvalues on unit circle
Hermitian operators have complex eigenvalues
Unitary operators are a special case of Hermitian operators
Anti-Hermitian operators cannot have eigenvalues
passive.explanation: For a matrix $A$: $(A^\dagger)_{ij} = A^*_{ji}$ (conjugate transpose). The spectrum theorem: a Hermitian operator on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space has a complete orthonormal eigenbasis. For infinite-dimensional spaces (quantum mechanics), self-adjoint operators require careful domain considerations (Stone-von Neumann theorem). Anti-Hermitian operators generate unitary groups: $U(t) = e^{iHt/\hbar}$ where $H = H^\dagger$ (Hermitian) makes $U$ unitary.

question: The spectral theorem for Hermitian operators states that a Hermitian operator $H$ on a finite-dimensional inner product space has an orthonormal eigenbasis $\{|n\rangle\}$. The resolution of identity and spectral decomposition are:

options: Any function of $H$ is $f(H) = \sum_n f(E_n)|n\rangle\langle n|$; the time evolution operator $e^{-iHt/\hbar} = \sum_n e^{-iE_n t/\hbar}|n\rangle\langle n|$
The spectral decomposition requires $H$ to be positive definite
$f(H) = f(\sum_n E_n)|n\rangle\langle n|$ — $f$ is applied to the sum, not individual eigenvalues
The spectral theorem fails for degenerate eigenvalues
passive.explanation: The spectral decomposition allows computing $e^H$, $\ln H$, $\sqrt{H}$, and any function of a Hermitian matrix by acting on eigenvalues. The resolution of identity (completeness relation) $\sum_n|n\rangle\langle n| = I$ is used to insert complete sets of states in quantum calculations. For continuous spectra (position/momentum): $\int|x\rangle\langle x|dx = I$ and $\langle x|p\rangle = e^{ipx/\hbar}/\sqrt{2\pi\hbar}$.

question: The commutator $[A, B] = AB - BA$ of two operators on a Hilbert space measures their non-commutativity. The canonical commutation relation for position $\hat{x}$ and momentum $\hat{p}$ is:

options: This relation is the foundation of quantum mechanics; it implies $\hat{p} = -i\hbar\partial/\partial x$ in position representation and leads to the Heisenberg uncertainty principle $\Delta x\,\Delta p \geq \hbar/2$
$[\hat{x},\hat{p}] = \hbar$ (without the factor of $i$)
The commutation relation holds only for free particles
$[\hat{x},\hat{p}] = 0$ in the classical limit $\hbar \to 0$
passive.explanation: $[\hat{x},\hat{p}] = i\hbar$ follows from $\hat{p} = -i\hbar\partial/\partial x$: $[x, -i\hbar\partial_x]f = -i\hbar(x\partial_x f - \partial_x(xf)) = -i\hbar(x\partial_x f - f - x\partial_x f) = i\hbar f$. The Robertson uncertainty: $\Delta A\Delta B \geq |\langle[A,B]\rangle|/2 = \hbar/2$ for $A = \hat{x}$, $B = \hat{p}$. The Poisson bracket $\{x,p\} = 1$ is the classical limit ($[\cdot,\cdot] \to i\hbar\{\cdot,\cdot\}$ as $\hbar \to 0$).

question: A Hilbert-Schmidt operator $A$ on a Hilbert space satisfies $\text{tr}(A^\dagger A) < \infty$. The Hilbert-Schmidt inner product between two operators is:

options: This makes the space of Hilbert-Schmidt operators itself a Hilbert space; density matrices (trace-class, positive semidefinite) form a convex subset; the Hilbert-Schmidt distance $\|\rho_1 - \rho_2\|_{\rm HS}$ measures distinguishability
The Hilbert-Schmidt inner product equals the usual vector inner product for matrices
All operators on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space are Hilbert-Schmidt
Hilbert-Schmidt operators must be invertible
passive.explanation: On finite-dimensional spaces, all operators are Hilbert-Schmidt. For infinite-dimensional spaces (e.g., harmonic oscillator), the position operator is unbounded and not Hilbert-Schmidt. In quantum information, the Hilbert-Schmidt inner product $\text{tr}(A^\dagger B)$ measures the overlap between quantum processes (quantum process tomography). The Frobenius norm $\|A\|_F = \sqrt{\text{tr}(A^\dagger A)}$ is the matrix analogue of the $L^2$ norm.
